1.synthesis: As beloved children, they are to be imitators of God and walk in love, as Christ loved them and gave himself up for them, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God (Ephesians 5:1–2). Sexual immorality and all impurity or covetousness must not even be named among them, as is proper among saints—nor filthiness, nor foolish talk, nor crude joking, but instead thanksgiving. For they may be sure that everyone who is sexually immoral or impure, or who is covetous—an idolater—has no inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and God (Ephesians 5:3–5). Let no one deceive them with empty words; the wrath of God comes upon the sons of disobedience. They are to take no part in their works (Ephesians 5:6–7). They were once darkness, but now they are light in the Lord—they should walk as children of light. The fruit of light is found in all that is good and right and true (Ephesians 5:8–9). Try to discern what is pleasing to the Lord; take no part in the unfruitful works of darkness but instead expose them (Ephesians 5:10–11). For it is shameful even to speak of the things they do in secret, but when anything is exposed by the light, it becomes visible. 'Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you' (Ephesians 5:14).
1.historical_context: The imitation-of-God call (v.1) draws on the Levitical holiness code ('be holy as I am holy,' Leviticus 11:44) but grounds it christologically: the mode of imitating God is specified as walking in love 'as Christ loved us and gave himself up for us' (v.2). The fragrant offering language (v.2) applies the priestly sacrificial vocabulary of the LXT (Numbers, Leviticus) to Christ's self-giving: his death is the ultimate sacrifice, fulfilling what the temple offerings anticipated. The covetousness = idolatry identification (v.5) is Paul's characteristic insight: covetousness displaces God as the ultimate object of desire and trust, making possessions the functional deity. The 'awake, O sleeper' citation (v.14) appears to be an early Christian hymn or baptismal proclamation rather than an OT citation—it summarizes the entire section's call to transition from darkness/death to light/life in Christ.
1.literary_structure: The section moves through three movements: the christological motivation for ethical holiness (vv.1–2), the list of vices to avoid with eschatological warning (vv.3–7), and the light-darkness metaphor applied to discernment and community life (vv.8–14). The light-darkness contrast (vv.8–14) reframes the earlier vice-avoidance in cosmological terms: the community that is 'light in the Lord' not only avoids darkness but exposes it—a more active role than mere avoidance. The hymnic citation of v.14 ('awake, O sleeper') provides the evangelistic or baptismal horizon: the calling to walk in light is the same call that brought them from death to life in the first place.
1.theological_application: 'Walk in love, as Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (v.2) is the ethical norm stated in its most demanding form: the standard of Christian love is the cross—the totality of self-giving for the other's sake. This standard makes every moral claim relative: the question in any situation is not 'what does the law require?' but 'what does love—Christ's kind of love—demand?' The eschatological warning (v.5: 'has no inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and God') is not a threat against believers who struggle but a warning against those who pursue the patterns of the old life as though the kingdom's arrival makes no claim on their behavior. The discernment call ('try to discern what is pleasing to the Lord,' v.10) assumes that the Spirit-led community, walking in light, has the moral perception to navigate complex situations—not by rule-application but by cultivated awareness of what pleases the Lord.
1.christology: 'Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (v.2) recasts the entire Levitical sacrificial system as a type of Christ's self-giving: the sweet aroma of the temple offerings was always anticipating the one offering that would exhaust the need for all sacrifice. The call to imitate God is immediately specified as imitating Christ—which means God's character is most fully disclosed in Christ's self-giving love. 'Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you' (v.14) identifies Christ as the light that illuminates the awakened: his resurrection is the light that calls the dead to life, and the community's walking in light is participation in the resurrection-light that Christ himself is and gives. The kingdom of 'Christ and God' (v.5) is the most explicit identification in Ephesians of Christ's lordship with God's own sovereignty.

15.synthesis: Look carefully how they walk, not as unwise but as wise, making the best use of the time, because the days are evil (Ephesians 5:15–16). Therefore do not be foolish but understand what the will of the Lord is. And do not get drunk with wine, for that is debauchery, but be filled with the Spirit, addressing one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody to the Lord with all the heart, giving thanks always and for everything to God the Father in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, submitting to one another out of reverence for Christ (Ephesians 5:17–21).
15.historical_context: The 'making the best use of the time' (exagorazomenoi ton kairon, v.16) is an eschatological idiom: the time is to be 'redeemed' or 'bought up' because the present evil age is running out and every moment matters. The contrast between wine and Spirit (v.18) probably alludes to Pentecost (Acts 2:13–18: the accusation of drunkenness, rebutted by reference to Joel's Spirit-promise) and to Dionysian cult-practices in Asia Minor where ecstatic wine-drinking was associated with divine possession. The Spirit-filling as the alternative: not wine-induced altered consciousness but Spirit-given overflow in psalms, hymns, spiritual songs, thanksgiving, and mutual submission. The five-fold Spirit-overflow (psalms, hymns, songs, thanksgiving to God, submission to one another) connects worship and ethics: the Spirit-filled community sings to the Lord and submits to one another—the same Spirit-fullness produces both liturgical and relational fruit.
15.literary_structure: The wisdom-walk appeal (vv.15–16) introduces the practical discernment section, which culminates in the Spirit-filling command (v.18) and its five consequential participles (addressing, singing, making melody, giving thanks, submitting, vv.19–21). Verse 21 ('submitting to one another out of reverence for Christ') is the hinge between the Spirit-filling section and the household code that follows (5:22–6:9): mutual submission is the Spirit's relational fruit, and the household relationships are specific applications of this general principle. The 'be filled with the Spirit' command is present passive imperative—ongoing and received, not a one-time achievement but a continual filling.
15.theological_application: 'Be filled with the Spirit' (v.18) is the positive command that governs all the ethical instruction of chapters 4–6: the behaviors commanded are the overflow of the Spirit's filling rather than moral achievements of the will. The contrast with wine-drunkenness is instructive: both wine and Spirit produce altered states of consciousness and behavior, but wine-drunkenness produces debauchery while Spirit-filling produces worship, thanksgiving, and mutual submission. The 'always and for everything' of v.20 extends thanksgiving beyond pleasant circumstances to the comprehensive gratitude that recognizes God's purposes in all things—the same thanksgivings that occur 'in all things' (1 Thessalonians 5:18) is here grounded in the Spirit's filling that transforms perception.
15.christology: 'Giving thanks always and for everything to God the Father in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ' (v.20)—thanksgiving is offered to the Father through the mediation of the Son: Christ's name is the ground and channel of access to the Father. The 'submitting to one another out of reverence for Christ' (v.21) is the christological motivation for mutual submission: the community submits to one another in recognition of Christ's own lordship over each member—to honor the other is to honor the Christ in whom each member belongs. The household code that follows (vv.22–6:9) will elaborate this submission in specific relational roles, but always with Christ as the supreme referent: wives submit 'as to the Lord' (v.22), husbands love 'as Christ loved the church' (v.25), children obey 'in the Lord' (6:1). Christ's self-giving is the norm by which all relations in the community are measured.

22.synthesis: Wives, submit to your own husbands as to the Lord. For the husband is the head of the wife even as Christ is the head of the church, his body, and is himself its Savior. Now as the church submits to Christ, so also wives should submit in everything to their husbands (Ephesians 5:22–24). Husbands, love your wives, as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word, so that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, that she might be holy and without blemish (Ephesians 5:25–27). In the same way husbands should love their wives as their own bodies—he who loves his wife loves himself. For no one ever hated his own flesh, but nourishes and cherishes it, just as Christ does the church, because we are members of his body (Ephesians 5:28–30). 'Therefore a man shall leave his father and mother and hold fast to his wife, and the two shall become one flesh' (Ephesians 5:31; citing Genesis 2:24). This mystery is profound—and Paul is speaking of Christ and the church. However, each of them should love his wife as himself, and the wife see that she respects her husband (Ephesians 5:32–33).
22.historical_context: The household code (haustafeln) of 5:22–6:9 reflects the conventional Greco-Roman household management form (wives/husbands, children/parents, slaves/masters), but Paul's christological reframing transforms the convention at every point. The wife's submission (v.22) is not an independent ethical command but flows from the mutual submission of 5:21 ('submitting to one another') and is immediately paralleled by the vastly larger ethical demand on the husband—love your wife as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her (vv.25–27). In the ancient world, this demand on the husband was culturally unprecedented: Greco-Roman household management expected the husband's authority without requiring the corresponding self-giving. The 'mystery' (mystērion, v.32) of Genesis 2:24 is now disclosed: the one-flesh union of husband and wife was always a type of the Christ-church union. Paul's theological interest in the marriage text is primarily ecclesiological: the husband-wife relationship illuminates the Christ-church relationship, and vice versa.
22.literary_structure: The marriage section is structured asymmetrically: two verses on wives (vv.22–24) and nine verses on husbands (vv.25–33). The imbalance is deliberate: the more radical ethical demand falls on the husband, who is called to the self-giving love of Christ rather than merely the assertive headship that the cultural default would have assumed. The Genesis citation (v.31) provides the scriptural anchor, and the 'mystery' disclosure (v.32) reveals the theological depth of the passage: Paul is not primarily teaching household management but disclosing the nature of the Christ-church union through the marriage analogy. The closing practical instruction (v.33: love and respect) reduces the profound theological vision to its practical relational form.
22.theological_application: The husband is called to love his wife 'as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her' (v.25)—the measure of husbandly love is Christ's cross. This means that any exercise of 'headship' that does not have the shape of self-giving for the other's sake has misread its own description: the head of the church is the one who gave himself up for her, who sanctifies and cleanses her, who presents her in splendor. The entire passage must be read within the mutual submission frame of v.21: the couple that practices the mutual submission of Spirit-filled community life, and within that frame takes on the specific callings of self-giving and responsive trust, embodies the mystery of the Christ-church union in domestic form. The 'mystery' of Genesis 2:24 disclosed in v.32 is one of the letter's major theological claims: marriage was designed from creation to be an icon of the union between Christ and his bride.
22.christology: Christ's relationship to the church is described in three actions: he gave himself up for her (v.25, the cross), he sanctifies and cleanses her by water and word (v.26, baptism and the ongoing work of the word), and he will present her to himself in splendor (v.27, the eschatological completion). These three define what christological 'headship' means: not authority that commands from above but love that gives itself, purifies, and brings to completion. 'We are members of his body' (v.30) is the basis of the husband's obligation to nourish and cherish: the church is Christ's own body, and what is done to the church is done to Christ—the husband's love for his wife participates analogically in Christ's love for his own body. The 'mystery' of Christ and the church (v.32) is the letter's deepest Christology: the union of Christ and the church is the ultimate reality that marriage images—and that marriage in turn illuminates.
